content:
# Genesis Chapter 42 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 25r–25v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_042_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_042_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0046 (page 42), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0047 (page 43)

---

## Go Down to Egypt

**1** When Jacob learned that there was grain in Egypt, he said to his sons, "Why are you just sitting here staring at each other?" **2** "I have heard," he said, "that there is grain in Egypt. Go down there and buy some for us, so that we may live and not die." **3** So ten of Joseph's brothers went down to buy grain in Egypt. **4** But Benjamin — Joseph's brother — Jacob did not send with his brothers, for he said, "I am afraid something might happen to him."

**5** So the sons of Israel came among those who were coming to buy grain, for the famine had reached the land of Canaan.

---

## The Dreams Begin to Come True

**6** Joseph was the governor over the land; he was the one who sold grain to all the people of the land. And Joseph's brothers came and bowed down before him with their faces to the ground. **7** When Joseph saw his brothers, he recognized them — but he acted as a stranger toward them and spoke to them harshly. "Where do you come from?" he demanded. "From the land of Canaan," they said, "to buy food."

**8** Joseph recognized his brothers, but they did not recognize him. **9** And Joseph remembered the dreams he had dreamed about them. He said to them, "You are spies! You have come to see where the land is exposed!"

---

## The Interrogation

**10** "No, my lord!" they said to him. "Your servants have come to buy food. **11** We are all sons of one man. We are honest men — your servants have never been spies."

**12** But he said to them, "No! You have come to see where the land is exposed."

**13** They replied, "We your servants are twelve brothers — sons of one man in the land of Canaan. The youngest is right now with our father, and one is gone."

**14** But Joseph said to them, "It is exactly as I said to you — you are spies! **15** Here is how you will be tested: as surely as Pharaoh lives, you will not leave this place unless your youngest brother comes here. **16** Send one of you to bring your brother, while the rest of you remain under arrest. This way your words will be tested — whether the truth is with you or not. If not, then as surely as Pharaoh lives, you are spies!" **17** And he held them in custody for three days.

---

## Three Days, and a Change of Terms

**18** On the third day, Joseph said to them, "Do this and you will live — I am a man who fears God. **19** If you are honest men, let one of your brothers remain confined here in the prison, while the rest of you go and carry grain back for your starving households. **20** Then bring your youngest brother to me, so that your words may be verified and you will not die." And they did so.

---

## The First Confession

**21** Then they said to one another, "We are truly guilty concerning our brother — we saw the distress of his soul when he pleaded with us, and we would not listen. That is why this anguish has come upon us."

**22** And Reuben answered them, "Didn't I tell you — don't sin against the boy? But you wouldn't listen. And now look — his blood is being demanded of us!"

**23** They did not know that Joseph was listening, because he had been using an interpreter with them. **24** He turned away from them and wept. Then he came back and spoke to them. He took Simeon from them and had him bound before their eyes.

---

## Grain, and a Secret

**25** Joseph then gave the order to fill their bags with grain, to restore every man's silver to his own sack, and to give them provisions for the journey. And that was done for them.

**26** They loaded their grain on their donkeys and set out from there. **27** When one of them opened his sack at the stopping place to give fodder to his donkey, he saw his silver right there in the mouth of his sack. **28** He said to his brothers, "My silver has been put back! There it is in my sack!" Their hearts sank, and they turned trembling to one another, saying, "What is this that God has done to us?"

---

## The Report to Jacob

**29** When they came to their father Jacob in the land of Canaan, they told him everything that had happened to them: **30** "The man who is lord of the land spoke harshly to us and took us for spies of the country. **31** But we said to him, 'We are honest men — we have never been spies. **32** We are twelve brothers, sons of our father. One is gone, and the youngest is right now with our father in the land of Canaan.'

**33** "Then the man who is lord of the land said to us, 'Here is how I will know you are honest men: leave one of your brothers with me, and take grain for your starving households and go. **34** But bring your youngest brother to me. Then I will know that you are not spies, that you are honest men — I will give you back your brother, and you will be free to move about the land.'"

---

## Terror in the Sacks

**35** As they were emptying their sacks, there was each man's bundle of silver in his sack. When they and their father saw the bundles of silver, they were afraid. **36** And their father Jacob said to them, "You have bereaved me of my children! Joseph is gone, and Simeon is gone, and now you would take Benjamin — everything is against me!"

---

## Reuben's Offer

**37** Then Reuben said to his father, "You may put my two sons to death if I do not bring him back to you. Put him in my hands, and I will bring him back to you."

**38** But Jacob said, "My son will not go down with you. His brother is dead, and he is the only one left. If something happens to him on the journey you are going to make, you will bring my gray head down to Sheol in grief."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### "Why are you staring at each other?" — the paralysis of guilt

**לָמָּה תִּתְרָאוּ** — "Why are you looking at one another?"

The verb **תִּתְרָאוּ** (*titraú*) is a Hithpael reflexive-reciprocal form of *ra'ah* — "to see" — meaning "to look at one another, to stare at each other." It is used nowhere else in Genesis. Jacob's question to his sons at the opening of the chapter is almost contemptuous in its brevity: *Why are you gazing at each other?* The word has the feeling of watching people who are frozen — ten men standing in a circle, eyes moving from face to face, collectively unable to act.

The question is purely practical on the surface: there is grain in Egypt, the famine is real, people are dying, go buy food. But the narrative has placed this moment directly after the seven years of famine have begun and after the whole earth has been shown streaming toward Joseph. The reader knows who is selling the grain in Egypt. The brothers do not. And the reader watches them stare at one another — unable to move, paralyzed — and wonders: is this only hunger that holds them?

There may be a darker edge to Jacob's question. The brothers are guilty men. They have been guilty for twenty-two years. Every time famine presses them toward Egypt, every time the name of Joseph's brothers is spoken, there is a weight that the narrative carries that the characters carry too, even if they have not spoken it aloud. They stand in a circle and stare at each other, and Jacob — who does not know what they know — snaps them out of it: *Stop staring. Go.*

---

### The bowing — the moment the dream becomes flesh

**וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ-לוֹ אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה** — "and they bowed down to him with their faces to the ground."

This is the verse the entire Joseph narrative has been building toward since Genesis 37:7: **וְהִנֵּה תְסֻבֶּינָה אֲלֻמֹּתֵיכֶם וַתִּשְׁתַּחֲוֶיןָ לַאֲלֻמָּתִי** — "and behold, your sheaves gathered around and bowed down to my sheaf." The dream of Joseph's youth — the dream that made his brothers hate him enough to sell him — is here enacted in history.

The word **וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ** (*vayyishtachavu*) — "they bowed down, they prostrated themselves" — is the identical root as the verb in the dream of 37:7. The form here is the Hithpael imperfect with *vav* consecutive, the narrative past of a completed action. They bowed. It happened. The dream is no longer a dream.

What makes the scene extraordinary is the silence around it. The narrative does not pause to announce the fulfillment. There is no exclamation point, no narrator's aside — "and thus the dream of Joseph was fulfilled." The fulfillment is simply there, visible to the reader who remembers chapter 37, embedded in the matter-of-fact account of a business transaction: the brothers came, they bowed, they requested grain. The dream's completion is narrated as administration.

The phrase **אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה** — "faces to the ground" — adds bodily precision: it is not a mere head-bow but full prostration, faces pressed to the earth. This is the *complete* bow, the bow reserved for royalty, for God, for total submission. Joseph's brothers are pressing their faces into the dirt of Egypt before a man they do not recognize. The irony is almost unbearable: the boy they threw in the pit is the man before whom they are now ground-level.

---

### Vayyitnakker — the stranger who is no stranger

**וַיִּתְנַכֵּר אֲלֵיהֶם** — "He made himself strange toward them / he acted as a stranger to them."

The verb **וַיִּתְנַכֵּר** (*vayyitnakker*) is the Hithpael of the root *n-k-r* — to be foreign, to be strange, to be unrecognizable. The Hithpael is reflexive: *he made himself strange, he acted as a foreigner to them.* Joseph is deliberately performing foreignness toward his own brothers — presenting himself as what he has become on the outside (an Egyptian official) while concealing what he has never stopped being on the inside (their brother).

This root *n-k-r* runs like a hidden thread through the entire Joseph narrative. In Genesis 37:32, when the brothers bring Joseph's blood-soaked coat to Jacob, they say: **הַכֶּר-נָא** (*hakker-na*) — "please identify / please recognize" — using the exact same root. The brothers placed a blood-stained garment before their father and asked him to recognize it; they were performing a false identification to cover a crime. In Genesis 38:25, Tamar says the same to Judah: **הַכֶּר-נָא** — "please identify" — placing before him the signet, cord, and staff he gave her, demanding that he recognize his own objects. Now, in chapter 42, Joseph *vayyitnakker* — he performs non-recognition, the deliberate act of making himself unrecognizable to the very men who once asked their father to "recognize" a garment.

The narrative irony is dense and precise: the man who was the object of a false identification scene in chapter 37 is now the subject of a self-performed non-recognition. He knows them; they do not know him. The power dynamic has completely inverted. Then, the brothers had all the information and Jacob had none, and they used that information gap to their advantage. Now, Joseph has all the information and his brothers have none, and he is using that gap — for the moment — toward ends the reader does not yet fully understand.

---

### Meraglim — the charge that cuts both ways

**מְרַגְּלִים אַתֶּם** — "You are spies!"

The word **מְרַגְּלִים** (*meraglim*) — spies, scouts, those who go to see the land's vulnerabilities — comes from the root *ragal*, "to go on foot, to travel about, to reconnoiter." The charge Joseph levels at his brothers is the standard accusation of an ancient Near Eastern border official toward strangers arriving from a foreign land in suspicious numbers. Ten men from Canaan, during a famine, all claiming to be brothers — the story is implausible enough to warrant interrogation.

But there is a second layer to the accusation that the Hebrew reader catches immediately. The word **עֶרְוַת הָאָרֶץ** (*ervat ha'arets*) — "the nakedness of the land, where the land is exposed" — is the phrase Joseph uses for what the spies have supposedly come to see. The root *ervah* — nakedness, exposure, vulnerability — is the same root at work in the Noah story (Gen 9:22–23) and in the sexual exposure laws of Leviticus. To see the *ervah* of a land is to see what it is ashamed to show, what makes it defenseless.

Joseph's charge is not only strategic (creating the pretext for detaining his brothers); it carries a resonance his brothers cannot hear. *You came to see where the land is exposed.* But they are the ones who exposed an innocent man — who stripped him of his coat, threw him in a pit, and sold him. The brothers are the ones who saw *Joseph's* nakedness and used it against him. The accusation is not false; it simply misidentifies the land.

---

### "We are twelve brothers — one is gone"

**שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר עֲבָדֶיךָ אַחִים אֲנַחְנוּ** — "We your servants are twelve brothers."

The brothers' defense of themselves before this Egyptian official includes a detail that serves as their credentials: *we are twelve brothers, sons of one man.* The family unity they assert is meant to argue against the spy charge — spies would be unrelated men sent by a foreign power, not a single family's sons.

But the number twelve is a wound that the brothers open before the man who caused the wound. They say: *twelve brothers. One is gone.* The phrase **הָאֶחָד אֵינֶנּוּ** — "the one is not" — is a construction of devastating compression: *the one is absent, is no more, does not exist here.* The word **אֵינֶנּוּ** (*einennu*) is the negation of existence: not "is elsewhere" but "is not." They report Joseph's absence the way one reports a death, because that is what they believe it is, or at least what they have been living with for twenty-two years.

Joseph is standing in front of them. He is the one "who is not." He hears his own non-existence reported in his presence. The scene is almost impossible to process: the absent brother, told he does not exist, by the men who made him absent, while he listens through an interpreter and watches their faces and controls his expression and decides what to do next. That his face does not break — that he manages to hold this — speaks to either extraordinary self-possession or extraordinary pain held behind a mask of power.

---

### "I fear God" — and why that matters in the mouth of an Egyptian official

**אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים אֲנִי יָרֵא** — "I am a man who fears God."

When Joseph revises his conditions on the third day — changing from "one goes, nine stay" to "nine go, one stays" — he prefaces the change with this statement: *do this and live, for I am a man who fears God.* The phrase **יְרֵא אֱלֹהִים** (*yiré Elohim*, one who fears God) is the language of covenantal ethics in the Hebrew Bible. It describes Abraham in chapter 22 — **כִּי עַתָּה יָדַעְתִּי כִּי-יְרֵא אֱלֹהִים אָתָּה** — "now I know that you fear God." It describes the midwives in Exodus 1:17 who refuse Pharaoh's order because "they feared God." Fear of God is the theological grounding for mercy extended to the vulnerable.

For the brothers, Joseph's statement comes from an Egyptian official they do not recognize, and it means: *I am bound by a moral code that compels me toward fairness, even toward foreigners.* It is the basis on which he releases nine of them rather than holding all ten. The mercy the brothers are receiving is framed by its source: not Egyptian generosity, not political prudence, but fear of the God who holds all human beings accountable.

The irony is total and silent: the man who fears God and is releasing them from prison is the man they sold into slavery. The mercy they are receiving comes from the victim of their crime. Joseph says *I fear God* — and in his mouth, that statement contains everything the brothers cannot yet know: I am still the man who was taught to fear God by the same father who taught you; I have not become someone else; the God of Jacob holds me still.

---

### Ashamim anachnu — the anatomy of the first confession

**אֲבָל אֲשֵׁמִים אֲנַחְנוּ עַל-אָחִינוּ** — "We are truly guilty concerning our brother."

This sentence — Genesis 42:21 — is one of the most theologically charged lines in the entire Joseph story. It is the first time anyone in the narrative explicitly names what the brothers did to Joseph as guilt. Not mistake, not misfortune, not misjudgment: **אֲשֵׁמִים** (*ashamim*), guilty. The word is the root of the *asham* — the guilt offering, the reparation offering of Leviticus — the sacrifice required when a person has committed an offense that caused real harm to another and now must be repaired.

The sentence has two additional elements of terrible precision. First: **בְּהִתְחַנְנוֹ אֵלֵינוּ** — "when he pleaded with us." Twenty-two years have passed and the brothers remember: he *pleaded*. The boy in the pit — seventeen years old — begged his brothers to release him, and they watched him and did not. The Hebrew root **חָנַן** (*chanan*) — to plead for grace, to beg for mercy — is the root of the prayer word *chinnun*, the desperate appeal of the powerless to those with power. Joseph made himself as small and as vulnerable as possible before his brothers, and they sat down and ate bread.

Second: **אֶת-צָרַת נַפְשׁוֹ** — "the distress of his soul." The word **צָרַת** (*tsarat*) — distress, anguish, the pressing in of suffering — combined with **נֶפֶשׁ** (*nefesh*, soul, living self) describes the totality of Joseph's condition in the pit. They saw his *nefesh* in *tsarah* — his whole living self in anguish. And they would not listen. The phrase is not an abstraction; it is a body memory. They saw it, they heard it, and they turned away.

The confession is not made to Joseph — it is made to each other, in Joseph's presence, through an interpreter he is using as cover, while he listens to every word. It is the confession of men who believe the confessee is dead and can never hear them. The fact that he hears it makes verse 24 — **וַיֵּבְךְּ** — "and he wept" — completely inevitable.

---

### Vayyibk — the first of Joseph's tears

**וַיֵּבְךְּ** — "And he wept."

Two words. The shortest sentence in the chapter — three letters in Hebrew — and one of the most moving in Genesis. Joseph has just heard his brothers confess what they did, confess that they saw his pleading and ignored it, confess that they have been carrying this guilt for decades. And the verse says simply: he turned away from them and wept.

The verb **וַיֵּבְךְּ** (*vayyibk*) from *bakah* — to weep, to cry — will recur several more times in the Joseph narrative: in 43:30, when he sees Benjamin; in 45:2, when he reveals himself; in 45:14–15, when he embraces his brothers; in 46:29, when he reunites with Jacob; in 50:1, when Jacob dies. Each weeping is different in its cause and its context. This first weeping — over his brothers' guilt-confession, heard in secret — is the purest.

What moves Joseph to tears here? Not the hardship — twenty-two years of slavery, prison, and forgotten promise have not made him weep. Not the dream fulfilled — he has just watched ten men prostrate themselves before him. What breaks him is the confession. He hears that they knew what they did was wrong. He hears that they carried it. He hears that the image of his pleading soul has stayed with them. And he weeps — privately, turned away, away from their sight — and then turns back and continues the test.

This structure — weeping privately, then returning to the test — is the emotional architecture of a man who is not yet ready. Something is being built in Joseph across the next several chapters before the revelation of chapter 45. Each encounter with his brothers, each piece of information he extracts, each test he administers, is preparation for a moment that requires him to be certain of something before he risks it. The weeping shows the reader what is happening inside; the return to the interrogation shows what he has decided is still necessary.

---

### The money in the sacks — the dread of gift

**וְהִנֵּה-כֶסֶף אִישׁ בְּפִי אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ** — "And there was each man's silver in the mouth of his sack!"

The return of the money is Joseph's own doing — the text explicitly says in verse 25 that he ordered it done — but its discovery by the brothers reads as supernatural shock. The man at the stopping place opens his sack to feed his donkey and finds his payment sitting there, as if the grain had refused to be purchased, as if Egypt had sent them away with both the food *and* the price they paid.

The brothers' response is immediate and theological: **מַה-זֹּאת עָשָׂה אֱלֹהִים לָנוּ** — "What is this that God has done to us?" They do not say: the Egyptian official made a mistake. They do not say: a servant miscounted. They say: God. The money in the sack is experienced as a divine act, and a frightening one — because men who are guilty and who have just confessed their guilt to one another naturally read everything threatening as punishment.

The return of the money is of course Joseph's act of provision — he is trying to sustain his family, he is giving them the grain and the money back. But it is also a trap, in the sense that when they arrive in Canaan with unreturned silver, they cannot simply go back to Egypt without explanation. The money creates the problem that will force the next conversation with Jacob and the next trip south. What looks like generosity is also a mechanism of the story's forward movement.

The deeper resonance: the silver that passed from the brothers' hands to Midianite merchants when Joseph was sold is now passing back into their hands from Joseph's. The twenty pieces of silver (37:28) that sealed his enslavement return to them — not as restitution, but as dread. They cannot spend it and they cannot ignore it. The money their guilt earned is back, and they do not know what it means.

---

### Jacob's lament — the grammar of loss

**אֹתִי שִׁכַּלְתֶּם יוֹסֵף אֵינֶנּוּ וְשִׁמְעוֹן אֵינֶנּוּ** — "You have bereaved me — Joseph is not, and Simeon is not."

Jacob's sentence at verse 36 is one of the great laments in biblical prose, and its power is almost entirely grammatical. The verb **שִׁכַּלְתֶּם** (*shikkalthem*) — "you have bereaved me, you have made me childless" — is the Pi'el intensive of *shakal*, the same root applied to a ewe bereaved of her lamb. The Pi'el form emphasizes the degree of loss: not merely "you took my children from me" but "you have made me a bereaved parent" — which in Hebrew culture is a condition, an identity, not just an event. A bereaved parent is *shakul* — permanently, constitutively bereft.

Then the list: **יוֹסֵף אֵינֶנּוּ** — "Joseph is not." **וְשִׁמְעוֹן אֵינֶנּוּ** — "and Simeon is not." The construction **אֵינֶנּוּ** (*einennu*) appears twice in the same verse — the same construction his sons used before Joseph in verse 13. There it was a report to an official about an absent person. Here it is a father's grief-stricken inventory of his losses, the two names followed by the single word that means *no longer exists here*. It is the most economical possible expression of bereavement.

Then: **וְאֶת-בִּנְיָמִן תִּקָּחוּ** — "and Benjamin you would take." The shift from perfect tense (what has happened: Joseph gone, Simeon gone) to the imperfect (what they are threatening to do: take Benjamin) creates a movement from past loss to anticipated loss. Jacob is not only grieving what he has lost; he is defending what he has left. He is, in his own calculus, fighting for the last child of Rachel who remains alive.

**עָלַי הָיוּ כֻלָּנָה** — "All these things are against me." The final clause is Jacob's most desperate claim. **עָלַי** (*alai*, upon me, against me) combined with **כֻלָּנָה** (*kullanah*, all of them, all these things) expresses a feeling of total assault — as if the universe itself has targeted Jacob for particular grief. Every loss points at him. Everything is against me.

Jacob does not know what the reader knows: that "all these things" are moving *toward* him, not against him. The structures of his grief — the missing sons, the food crisis, the forced trip to Egypt — are the very mechanisms by which his family will be preserved and reunited. The father who sees only the front face of his suffering has not yet seen the back of it.

---

### Reuben's impossible offer — the firstborn's failed remedy

**אֶת-שְׁנֵי בָנַי תָּמִית** — "You may put my two sons to death."

Reuben's offer is startling for its violence and its futility. He says to his father: put Benjamin in my care, and if I do not return him to you, kill my two sons. The offer has a kind of desperate sincerity — this is the most he can offer, the pledge of his own children's lives — but it is also absurd. If Benjamin does not return, Jacob will have lost *three* grandchildren instead of one. The offer does not compensate; it compounds.

Reuben is the firstborn who lost his birthright after the incident with Bilhah (35:22). He is the brother who, back in chapter 37, tried to save Joseph by suggesting the pit rather than murder, intending to come back and rescue him — and who returned to find the pit empty (37:29). He is the brother who said to his brothers then, when Joseph was sold: **וַיַּעַן רְאוּבֵן אֹתָם לֵאמֹר הֲלוֹא אָמַרְתִּי אֲלֵיכֶם לֵאמֹר אַל-תֶּחֶטְאוּ בַיֶּלֶד** — "Did I not tell you, do not sin against the boy?" (42:22). Reuben is a man who sees what is right and fails to enforce it.

The pattern holds here: the offer is well-intentioned and structurally defective. Reuben tries to solve the problem of Benjamin's safety by staking his own children. But Jacob is not persuaded — the offer is not compelling because Reuben is not Judah. Jacob's refusal is immediate and absolute. He will not send Benjamin with Reuben. The chapter ends with the door closed.

This contrast — Reuben's failed offer here versus Judah's successful offer in chapter 44, where Judah stakes himself rather than his sons — is a character study built across three chapters. What Reuben cannot do in chapter 42, Judah will accomplish in chapter 44. The trajectory of the Joseph narrative is also the trajectory of Judah's moral transformation, from the man who proposed selling his brother for twenty pieces of silver (37:26–27) to the man who offers himself as a slave in his brother's place (44:33).

---

### "My gray head down to Sheol" — the weight of Jacob's love

**וְהוֹרַדְתֶּם אֶת-שֵׂיבָתִי בְּיָגוֹן שְׁאֹלָה** — "You will bring my gray head down to Sheol in grief."

The phrase **שֵׂיבָה** (*seivah*, gray/white hair, old age as embodied in the whitening head) appears in Jacob's speech here and will appear again in the parallel phrase of 44:29 and 44:31. It is the sign of extreme age — of a man who has lived long enough to see many of his children die, or so he believes. The gray head is the body's record of loss.

**שְׁאוֹל** (*Sheol*) — the realm of the dead, the underworld where the dead go in Hebrew cosmology — is where Jacob says he is being led. The movement **הוֹרִיד** (*horid*, to bring down, to make descend) is the same verb used for going down to Egypt (*yarad*) and for Joseph being brought down into Egypt. The narrative is full of descent: brothers go down to Egypt, Joseph was brought down, and now Jacob fears being brought *down* to Sheol, the final descent.

The phrase **בְּיָגוֹן** (*beyagon*, in grief, in sorrow) is the same word used in 37:35 when Jacob refused consolation after seeing the bloody coat: **וַיְמָאֵן לְהִתְנַחֵם** — "he refused to be comforted" — saying *I will go down to my son in Sheol grieving.* The exact same phrase is echoing here, twenty-two years later. Jacob has not healed. The grief of Joseph's supposed death is still present, still fresh enough that the language for it is still the same. He is a man who has been living on the edge of Sheol for over two decades and is afraid of one more push.

---

### Structure of the chapter — the pivot of recognition

Genesis 42 is structured around a central irony that controls every scene: the characters are in a recognition drama in which only one party has all the information. The structure operates in two movements:

**Movement 1 — The Egyptian Administration (vv.1–20):** Everything appears to be about grain management and border security. Jacob sends his sons; the brothers bow; an official interrogates and detains them. The reader sees the bowing as dream-fulfillment; the brothers see it as submission to foreign authority. Joseph sees his brothers; the brothers see an Egyptian governor. The information gap is total and controlled.

**Movement 2 — The Interior Truth Surfaces (vv.21–38):** The brothers confess to each other; Joseph weeps privately; Simeon is bound; money is returned. The brothers return to Jacob and narrate the Egyptian episode; Jacob laments. The external drama of grain commerce is now shown to be an internal drama of guilt, grief, and family fracture.

The structural hinge is verse 24 — **וַיֵּבְךְּ** — which is both the midpoint of the chapter and the moment where Joseph's private interior breaks through his public face. After this, the chapter traces the ripple effects of the encounter outward: to the sacks, to the road, to Canaan, to Jacob's house. The weeping is the pivot.

---

### Manuscript observations

The Leningrad Codex folio 25r (GEN_042_page_001.jpg), photographed as part of the West Semitic Research Project by Bruce E. Zuckerman, opens with the conclusion of Genesis 41 in the right column and transitions into Genesis 42 approximately a third of the way down. A clear parasha section marker is visible at the chapter transition, the standard Masoretic **פ** (petuhah, open section) notation signaling the division between units. The Tiberian vocalization throughout the Genesis 42 text is consistent with the careful Masoretic hand visible throughout the LC.

The key word **וַיִּתְנַכֵּר** (*vayyitnakker*) in verse 7 is legible with the characteristic Hithpael prefix *vav-yod-tav* and the *nkr* root, the *dagesh* forte in the *nun* marking the Hithpael stem visible in the pointing. The triple occurrence of the root *n-k-r* across the verse — the same root as *hakker-na* in 37:32 and 38:25 — is visible as a pattern of consonants across the column.

The phrase **מְרַגְּלִים אַתֶּם** (*meraglim atem*, "you are spies") in verse 9 is legible in the left column of folio 25r (page 001), the *dagesh forte* in the *gimel* of *meraglim* visible in the pointing.

The pivotal verse 21 — **אֲבָל אֲשֵׁמִים אֲנַחְנוּ** ("we are truly guilty") — appears in folio 25v (GEN_042_page_002.jpg), visible in the right column. The word **אֲשֵׁמִים** (*ashamim*) — the guilt-related plural participle — carries its distinctive *aleph-shin-mem* root with Tiberian pointing, the *qamets* under the *aleph* and *patach* under the *shin* clear in the manuscript.

The weeping verse **וַיֵּבְךְּ** (v.24) is notable in the manuscript for its extreme brevity — the shortest sentence in the chapter — visible in the right column of folio 25v (page 002) as a very short word cluster standing nearly alone.

Jacob's lament in verse 36 — **אֹתִי שִׁכַּלְתֶּם** and **עָלַי הָיוּ כֻלָּנָה** — is visible in the left column of folio 25v (page 002), the Pi'el form **שִׁכַּלְתֶּם** with its characteristic *dagesh forte* in the *kaf* readable in the pointed text.

The final verse — **וְהוֹרַדְתֶּם אֶת-שֵׂיבָתִי בְּיָגוֹן שְׁאֹלָה** — closes the chapter in the left column of folio 25v, the word **שְׁאֹלָה** (*she'olah*, to Sheol) with the directional *-ah* suffix (indicating motion toward) and the distinctive *shewa* under the *shin* visible in the manuscript.

Notable LXX (Vaticanus) variants for Genesis 42:

**V. 6** — Vaticanus page 42 (pa_0042) carries the opening verses of Genesis 42, continuing from the end of chapter 41 without paragraph break in the uncial text. The LXX renders **וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ-לוֹ אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה** as **προσεκύνησαν αὐτῷ ἐπὶ πρόσωπον ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν** — "they bowed down before him, face upon the earth" — using the verb *proskuneō* (προσκυνέω), which in the Greek NT becomes the standard word for worship. The LXX translator's choice means that the fulfillment of Joseph's dream of the brothers' bowing carries the same verbal register as divine worship. The brothers "worship" Joseph not idolatrously but in the exact vocabulary the Greek Bible will use for prostration before God.

**V. 7** — The word **וַיִּתְנַכֵּר** is rendered in Vaticanus as **ἠλλοτριοῦτο** (*ēllotriōuto*) — "he was estranged, he made himself foreign/other" — from *allotrios* (foreign, belonging to another). The LXX translator captures the self-alienating quality of Joseph's performance: he made himself *other* to his own brothers, performed the alienation that Egypt had performed on him. The Vaticanus hand on page 42 is relatively clear at this point, the uncial letters of this rare Hithpael equivalent legible.

**V. 21** — The key confession **אֲשֵׁמִים אֲנַחְנוּ** is rendered by Vaticanus as **ἔνοχοί ἐσμεν** (*enochoi esmen*) — "we are guilty, we are liable." The word **ἔνοχος** (*enochos*) is the Greek legal term for being under obligation, being held to account, being culpable — the same word used in the NT for being "guilty of" the body and blood of Christ (1 Cor 11:27) and "guilty of eternal sin" (Mark 3:29). The LXX translator rendered the brothers' confession with the language of legal culpability — they are not just sorry, they are *held*.

The phrase **τὴν θλῖψιν τῆς ψυχῆς αὐτοῦ** (*tēn thlipsin tēs psychēs autou*) — "the affliction of his soul" — for **אֶת-צָרַת נַפְשׁוֹ** is significant. The word **θλῖψις** (*thlipsis*) is the standard NT word for tribulation, suffering, and affliction — "in the world you will have *thlipsis*" (John 16:33). The LXX has used the great tribulation-word of the NT for the anguish Joseph experienced in the pit, making him typologically resonant with every sufferer the NT will describe as undergoing *thlipsis*.

**V. 24** — Vaticanus renders the weeping simply: **ἀποστραφεὶς δὲ ἀπ᾽ αὐτῶν ἔκλαυσεν** — "and turning away from them, he wept." The aorist participle **ἀποστραφείς** (*apostrapheis*, having turned away) and the aorist **ἔκλαυσεν** (*eklausen*, he wept) preserve the same syntax as the Hebrew: first the turning, then the tears. The Vaticanus scribe on page 43 (pa_0043) — visible in the opening column of that page — has maintained the brevity of the sentence. Verse 24's weeping in Greek, as in Hebrew, is a very short thing in the text that carries an enormous weight of meaning.

**V. 38** — The final verse's **שְׁאוֹלָה** (*she'olah*, to Sheol) is rendered by Vaticanus as **εἰς ᾍδου** (*eis Hadou*) — "to Hades." This is the standard LXX translation of Sheol: the Greek underworld name *Hadēs* substituted for the Hebrew *she'ol*. The theological content is identical — a place of the dead to which the living descend — but the cultural register shifts: for Greek readers, Hades was a mythologically elaborated realm, not the relatively underdeveloped *she'ol* of early Hebrew thought. The LXX translation makes Jacob's grief speak to Greek as well as Hebrew readers: *my gray head down to Hades in sorrow* — the aged, grieving father at the lip of the underworld, holding back by sheer love of the last boy.

The chapter marker visible at the top of Vaticanus page 43 — **CAP. XLVIII** in the Vaticanus chapter numbering — confirms that the Vaticanus scribal tradition placed the Genesis 42–43 boundary within this page. The uncial text on page 43 carries both the middle of Genesis 42 (the brothers' report to Jacob, Jacob's lament) and the opening of Genesis 43 in continuous, unparagraphed text, consistent with the Vaticanus LXX's presentation of the Joseph narrative as a flowing whole.

---

Folio 25v (GEN_042_page_002.jpg, PDF page 49) carries Genesis 42:21–42:38 and the beginning of Genesis 43.
